title: egyptian_civilization_An_Account_of_Egypt_by_Herodotus
paragraphs: The work, as we have it, is divided into nine books, named after the nine Muses. Despite appearing credulous to modern eyes, he takes great care to distinguish between what he knows through his own observations and what he has inferred or been told. Psammetichos heard that his son Amasis was crying out in pain. He asked his priest, who told him that the child's cries were a sign of a great calamity. The priest said that if the child could speak, it would reveal the future fate of Egypt.

As time passed, two years went by, and the shepherd continued his ways. When he finally opened the door and stepped inside, both children fell to their knees, imploring him with tears in their eyes and uttering a single word: "Bekos." As for raising the children, this is what I mentioned earlier. Additionally, during my conversation with the priests of Hephaestus in Memphis, I learned other relevant information.

The Egyptians were the first to introduce appellations for the twelve gods. The Hellenes adopted this practice from them. They also built altars, images, and temples for the gods, as well as engraved figures on stones. In fact, the Egyptians demonstrated these practices through actual evidence, which they had observed in history. From Heliopolis, however, as you ascend, Egypt becomes narrower. On one side, a mountain range belonging to Arabia stretches along its edge, running northwards towards midday and southwards towards the setting sun, without interruption, until it reaches the Erythraian Sea. It is in this range that the stone quarries used for cutting stone for the pyramids at Memphis are located.

On this side, the mountain ends where I have stated, and then takes a turn back. It is at its widest point, as I was informed, that the journey lasts two months, stretching from east to west. The borders that face eastward are said to produce frankincense. Egypt was broad again. The priests also provided me with a strong proof concerning this land. They explained that during King Moiris' reign, whenever the Nile River reached a height of eight cubits, it would water Egypt below Memphis. It wasn't until nine hundred years had passed since Moiris' death that I heard these things from the priests. However, they now claim that unless the river rises to sixteen cubits or at least fifteen, it will not overflow the land.

The Egyptians did not emerge at the same time as the Delta region, as described by the Ionians. Rather, they existed from the dawn of humanity, with their land advancing over time. As a result, many remained in their original settlements, while others migrated downward to lower regions. The Oracle of Ammon's response corroborates my assertion that Egypt extends as far as I have described it in my account. This was revealed to me after I had already developed my own understanding of Egypt's scope. For those living in Marea and Apis, cities bordering Egypt on Libya, who believed themselves to be Libyans rather than Egyptians, and were also burdened by the rules of religious service due to their desire not to be excluded from consuming beef, they sent a message to Ammon stating that they had no connection with the Egyptians, as they resided outside the Nile Delta and shared nothing with them. They requested that it be lawful for them to eat whatever they pleased without distinction.

The god, however, did not permit them to do so. Instead, he said that the land belonged to Egypt, where the Nile River flowed and watered it. Those who lived in the city of Elephantine, located beneath the city, drank from that river. Some Hellenes who sought distinction through cleverness have described this water in three different ways. Two of these accounts I consider unnecessary to discuss further than their nature. One describes the Etesian Winds as the reason for the river's rise, which occurs when they prevent the Nile from flowing into the sea. The second method demonstrates greater ignorance than previously discussed. It's even more astonishing to explain. The river creates these effects because it originates from the ocean, and the ocean surrounds the entire globe.

The first and greatest evidence is provided by the winds, which blow hot from these regions. The second evidence lies in the fact that this land is always rainless and frost-free. However, once snow has fallen, it is inevitable that rain will follow within five days. This means that if it snows in those parts, rain must also occur there. The third piece of evidence comes from the people who inhabit this region, whose dark skin tone is a result of the intense heat. If, however, after I have identified flaws in the opinions presented, I must express my own opinion on the uncertain matters, I shall explain to you in my mind the reason for the Nile's increase during the summer. If one were to express the matter briefly, all has now been stated. Whatever region a god approaches most and stands directly above, it can be reasonably assumed that this area is most in need of water, and its natural streams and rivers have dried up the most.

The Sun does not send away all the water of the Nile every year; instead, it allows some to remain with itself. When winter becomes milder, the Sun returns to its place among the heavens. From that time on, it draws equally from all rivers. In the meantime, they flow in large volumes, as rainwater mixes with them in great quantities. This occurs because their country receives rainfall during this period and is filled with torrential streams. They are weak in summer, however, as the showers of rain do not help them, and they are also attracted to the sun.

The Nile, however, is unique among rivers, as it does not receive rainfall and is instead drawn by the Sun. As a result, its flow is significantly reduced during the winter months compared to the summer months. In the summer, the Nile's flow is balanced with that of other waters, but in the winter, it bears the sole responsibility for its flow. To me, however, this man seemed not to be speaking seriously when he said he had certain knowledge of it. He stated that there were two mountains, with their peaks reaching a sharp point, situated between Syene, in Thebes' district, and Elephantine. The names of the mountains were Crophi and Mophi. The sources of the Nile flowed from the mountain range in the middle. Half of the water flowed to Egypt, while the other half went to Ethiopia.

Psammetichos, the king of Egypt, was put on trial regarding the depth of the source. He claimed that he had a rope measuring thousands of fathoms, which he lowered into the well, but it reached no bottom. If Since no text was given, I will also return nothing. After this, you will disembark and begin your 40-day journey by land. Sharp rocks protrude from the water, and numerous reefs make navigation impossible for vessels.

After passing through this country, you will embark on another vessel and sail for 12 days. Then, you will arrive at a great city called Meroe. This city is said to be the mother-city of all other Ethiopians. The people who live there worship only Zeus and Dionysos, and they show great reverence for these gods. They have a temple dedicated to Zeus where an oracle is located, and when the god speaks through prophecy, they go to war whenever he commands them to do so. Hearing this, Psammetichos set forth in pursuit. When he came up with them, he entreated them much and endeavoured to persuade them not to desert the gods of their country and their children and wives. Upon this, one of them pointed to his private member and said that wherever this was, there they would have both children and wives.

But what comes after that point no one can clearly say; for this land is a desert due to the burning heat. As for Etearchos' account, let us consider only what has been said here, except that, according to the men of Cyrene, he claimed that the Nasamonians returned safely home and that the people they had visited were all wizards. However, as much as was possible to learn about its course through diligent inquiry, it is known that it runs into Egypt.

The Nile. In Egypt, I shall make my report at length, for it boasts an abundance of wonders that surpass those of any other land. Its works, too, are numerous and impressive, leaving no aspect unexplored. This is why further details about its greatness will be provided. In this way, the beast is tested. Their appointed manner of sacrifice is as follows: They lead the sealed beast to the altar where they are sacrificing, kindle a fire, pour libations of wine over the altar so that it runs down upon the victim, and call upon the god. Then, they cut the beast's throat and sever its head from its body. The body then of the beast they flay, but upon the head they make many imprecations first. Those who have a market and Hellenes sojourning among them for trade carry it to the market-place and sell it. In contrast, those without Hellenes cast it away into the river. This is the form of imprecation they utter upon the heads, praying that if any evil befalls either themselves or the land of Egypt in general, it may come rather upon this head.

They make their sacrifice after fasting. While the offerings are being burned, they all mourn by beating themselves. When they finish, they depart as a feast with what was left unburned from the sacrifice. The men of Thebes and those who follow their example claim that this custom was established among them for a specific reason. According to legend, Heracles wanted to meet Zeus, but Zeus did not want to be seen by him. Eventually, when Heracles persisted in his request, Zeus devised a plan. He flayed a ram, held the head of the ram in front of himself, and then wore the fleece over it. With this device, he was able to appear before Zeus without actually showing himself. The Egyptians depict Zeus with the face of a ram, as do the Ammonians, who follow their example. The Ammonians are settlers from both Egypt and Ethiopia, speaking a language that combines elements of both tongues. In my opinion, it is this god that the Egyptians call Zeus _Amun_.

The Thebans do not sacrifice rams but hold them sacred. On one day in the year, on the feast of Zeus, they cut up a single ram in the same manner as before, flay it, and cover its skin with an image of Zeus. They then bring another image of Heracles to accompany it. Heracles is not an ancient Egyptian god. He was a demigod in Greek mythology, the son of Zeus and a mortal woman, Alcmene. In fact, however, he is often associated with Egypt due to his worship by the Egyptians during the Ptolemaic period. The Greeks believed that Heracles was born in Thebes, Greece, but the Egyptians revered him as a god, particularly during the reign of Pharaoh Amasis II (570-526 BCE). He did not take the entire thing into account, but rather those wise men who followed him were able to convey its significance more fully.

Afterwards, when much time had elapsed, they learned from Egypt the names of the gods, all except Dionysos, whose name they learned long afterwards. Later, the Pelasgians consulted the Oracle at Dodona about the names, for this prophetic seat is considered to be the most ancient among the Hellenes' oracles, and it was the only one at that time. The things that come first are said by the priestesses of Dodona, while the others, those concerning Hesiod and Homer, I will discuss later.

The priestesses of Dodona shared their knowledge with me. The eldest was Promeneia, followed by Timarete, and the youngest was Nicandra. The other inhabitants of Dodona who were involved in the temple's affairs corroborated their stories. The women were called doves by the people of Dodona because they were perceived as barbarians and their voices sounded like those of birds. However, after some time, it is said that the dove spoke with a human voice, which is when the woman began to communicate in a way that could be understood. As long as she spoke in a foreign tongue, her words still seemed to resemble the sounds of a bird's voice. If she were truly a dove, how could she then speak with a human voice? They celebrate a festival in every city along the riverbank, and when they arrive at Bubastis, they hold a grand celebration with great sacrifices. More wine is consumed on this festival than throughout the entire rest of the year.

At the times when they gather together at Sais for their sacrifices, a certain night brings them together. They kindle many lamps in the open air around the houses. The lamps are saucers filled with salt and oil mixed, and the wick floats on the surface, burning throughout the night. This festival is called _Lychnocaia_ (the lighting of lamps). The few remaining individuals who have been left behind attempt to draw a cart with four wheels, which bears the shrine and the image within. The other priests stationed at the gateway try to prevent it from entering, but the men under a vow come to the god's assistance and strike them, while the others defend themselves. There was a fierce battle with staffs, and they broke each other's heads. I believe many were killed by their injuries, but the Egyptians claimed no one died.

If nothing was given to you, return nothing.

(Note:  It is written in a formal and somewhat archaic style.) They devise a plan as follows: either by force or secretly, they remove the young from the females and kill them. However, after killing them, they do not consume them. The females, deprived of their young and craving more, then approach the males, as it is an animal that is fond of its offspring. When a fire breaks out, the cats appear to be possessed by a divine force. The Egyptians stand at intervals, watching over the cats without attempting to put out the flames. As the cats slip through or leap over the men, they jump into the fire. This phenomenon leads to great mourning among the Egyptians.

Cats are taken to sacred buildings in the city of Bubastis when they die, where they are embalmed before being buried. Dogs, however, are buried in their own cities within sacred tombs, not alongside people. Ichneumons are also buried in a similar manner as dogs. The shrewmice, however, and the hawks carry away to the city of Buto, while the ibises go to Hermopolis. The bears, which are rarely seen, and the wolves, not much larger in size than foxes, bury them on the spot where they are found lying. The nature of a crocodile is as follows: during the four coldest months, this creature eats nothing. It has four feet and belongs to both land and water. On land, it produces eggs that hatch, and for most of the day, it remains on dry ground. At night, however, it spends its time in the river, where the water is warmer than the open air and dew.

Let us consider only what has been said thus far regarding sacred animals. They consume quails and ducks, as well as small birds, without cooking them first, after curing them. All other creatures belonging to the bird or fish class, except those set aside by them as sacred, are eaten either roasted or boiled. It is clear that they have sung this song for an immeasurable amount of time, and in the Egyptian language, Linos is referred to as Maneros.

They are not in agreement with any of the Hellenes. Instead of addressing each other on the roads, they show reverence, lowering their hands to their knees. They wear tunics of linen about their legs with fringes, which they call calasiris. Above these, they have garments of white wool thrown over. Woolen garments are not taken into the temples, and they are not buried with the dead, as this is forbidden by religion. People have discovered portents more often than anyone else. When a portent occurs, they observe and record the outcome. If something similar happens again in the future, they believe the outcome will be similar as well.

The third kind of embalming, by which bodies are prepared for those who have limited means, involves cleansing the abdomen with a purgative. The body is then preserved for embalming during a period of seventy days. After this time, the body is returned to the individuals responsible for transporting it away. Wives of men of high rank were not immediately prepared for burial. Nor were women considered particularly beautiful or of higher esteem than others. Instead, they were only allowed to be embalmed on the third or fourth day after their death, rather than sooner. People who live off fish alone catch them, clean them, and dry them in the sun. When they're dry, they eat them.

Fish that swim in schools are not much produced in rivers, but are bred in lakes. They follow this pattern: When they feel the urge to breed, they swim out in schools towards the sea. The males lead the way, releasing their sperm as they go, while the females follow and swallow it up, becoming impregnated. Once they're full of young in the sea, each school returns to its own habitats. The same principle no longer applies, but the females now take the lead. They follow in shoals, just like the males before them, laying eggs one or two at a time. The males then follow, swallowing their eggs whole. These grains are fish. The surviving grains give rise to new fish, which are then bred up.

They tend to stay near the shore because when swimming towards the sea, they remain on the left side of the river, and when swimming back upstream, they also stick to the same side, getting as close to the bank as possible, due to a natural fear of straying from their path. These boats cannot sail up the river unless there is a very fresh wind blowing. Instead, they are towed from the shore downstream. However, their travel follows this pattern: they have a door-shaped crate made of tamarisk wood and reed mats sewn together. Additionally, a stone weighing about two talents is bored with a hole; the boatman then lets the crate float in front of the boat, secured with a rope, while the stone drags behind, tied to another rope. The crate then, as the force of the stream presses upon it, goes on swiftly and draws on the baris, while the stone dragged after it, sunk deep in the water, keeps its course straight.

When this occurs, they cross over the plain rather than following the river's channels. For instance, sailing up from Naucratis to Memphis brings the passage close to the pyramids. However, even in this case, the usual route differs, passing near the point of the Delta and the city of Kercasoros. If you travel across the plain from the sea to Naucratis, via Canobos, you will pass by Anthylla and the city named after Archander. After this man, the priests enumerated to me from a papyrus roll the names of other kings. They included three hundred and thirty in total. Among these, eighteen were Ethiopians, one was a woman, and the rest were men who shared Egyptian ancestry. The name of the female ruler matched that of the Babylonian queen, Nitocris. They said that she desired to avenge her brother, who was killed by the Egyptians when he was their king. After killing him, they gave his kingdom to her. She sought revenge, and with cunning, she eliminated many of the Egyptians.

As they dined, she allowed the river to flood their table by a hidden passage. As for the other kings, they told me of no great works produced by them. They claimed to have no renown except that of their last ruler, Moiris. He, they said, had built a gateway as a memorial to himself, facing north towards the wind. Additionally, he had dug a lake, about which I will describe later. The lake's circumference is approximately 1,000 furlongs. They produced these works.

The king who succeeded them was Sesostris. When I considered the matter, I asked both the Colchians and the Egyptians. The Colchians remembered the Egyptians better than the Egyptians did the Colchians. However, the Egyptians claimed to believe that the Colchians were part of Sesostris' army. That I conjectured myself not only because they are dark-skinned and have curly hair, but also because the Colchians, Egyptians, and Ethiopians alone among all human races have practiced circumcision since its inception. The Egyptians and Ethiopians are unable for me to determine which one learned from the other. However, it is clear that this custom is very ancient. Nevertheless, I believe that other nations acquired it through contact with the Egyptians. For example, the Phoenicians, who have trade relations with Hellas, no longer follow Egyptian customs in this regard and do not circumcise their children.

Sesostris's sons were burned alive, but two of them perished in this manner. The others escaped unharmed. The king divided his land because some Egyptians, whose cities were inland and not near the river, lacked access to fresh water when the river's flow decreased. They obtained brackish water from wells instead. After escaping the affliction of his eyes, he made offerings at every renowned temple. Notably, he dedicated exceptional works at the Sun Temple, including two massive stone obelisks, each carved from a single block, measuring 100 cubits in length and 8 cubits in breadth.

Proteus questioned him about how he had obtained Helen. When Alexander gave an inaccurate account and lied, those who had petitioned him were convinced of his deceit. They recounted the entire story of the wrong committed. Now therefore depart, for I have deemed it of great importance not to be a slayer of innocent travelers. It is also evident from these lines and this passage that the "Cyprian Epic" was not written by Homer, but by another author. The text states that on the third day after leaving Sparta, Alexander arrived at Ilion with Helen, accompanied by a "gently-blowing wind and a smooth sea." In contrast, the Iliad describes him as wandering from his course when he brought her.

The Hellenes, however, thought they were being mocked by them and besieged the city until they finally took it. When they had taken the wall and did not find Helen, but heard the same story as before, they then believed the first tale and sent Menelaus himself to Proteus. Menelaus, however, proved ungrateful to the Egyptians after being dealt with in such a manner. When he set sail to leave, contrary winds prevented him from proceeding, and as this situation persisted for a long time, he devised an impious act; he took two children of native Egyptians and sacrificed them. When it was known that he had done so, he became abhorred. Pursued, he escaped and fled to Libya in his ships. The Egyptians could not say where he went afterwards.

They discovered some of this information through inquiry, while the rest - specifically what occurred in their own country - was based on reliable and verified knowledge. The kingdom was not coming to Alexander next, so when Priam grew old, he had control of the government. However, Hector, who was older and more mature than him, would have received it after Priam's death. It was not right for him to allow his brother to continue with his wrongdoing, as great evils were about to befall both him privately and the other Trojans in general. In truth, however, they lacked the power to restore Helen. The Hellenes did not believe them, even though they spoke the truth. For as I declare my opinion, the divine power intended to cause them to perish utterly and make it evident to men that great wrongs are met with great punishments from the gods.

After Proteus, Rhampsinitos received the kingdom in succession. As a memorial to himself, he built a gateway to the temple of Hephaistos, facing west. In front of the gateway, he erected two statues, twenty-five cubits tall. The statue on the north side was called Summer by the Egyptians, while the one on the south side was called Winter. To this Summer statue, they paid reverence and made offerings, whereas to the Winter statue, they performed the opposite rituals. This king, they said, accumulated an enormous wealth of silver, which none of the kings born after him could match or even approach. He wanted to safeguard his riches and built a chamber of stone, with one wall facing outwardly towards his palace. The builder, however, had ulterior motives and devised a plan as follows: he placed one of the stones in such a way that it could be easily removed from the wall by two men or even by one person alone. When the chamber was finished, the king stored his money in it. Later, as the builder neared the end of his life, he summoned his two sons and shared with them how he had planned the construction of the treasury, all in anticipation of their future well-being.

As the mother struggled to care for her surviving son, he attempted to persuade her by saying many things. However, she remained unswayed. To achieve his goal, he devised a plan: He filled some animal hides with wine and placed them on the backs of donkeys, then drove them towards the guards who were standing near the corpse. As he approached, he drew two or three of the wine-filled hides closer to the guards and loosened the ropes that held them in place. He then, as it was now far on in the night, first took down the body of his brother. He shaved the right cheeks of all the guards in mockery. Afterward, he placed the dead body upon the asses and drove them away home, having fulfilled what his mother had commanded him to do. When the news reached the king, he was initially astonished by the man's ingenuity and bravery. He subsequently ordered an announcement to be made in all the cities, offering a full pardon to the thief and promising a substantial reward if the individual came before him.

The priests wove a robe on the very day of the festival. They then blindfolded one of them with a cloth and led him to the path leading to the Temple of Demeter. The priests departed, leaving him there. The priest, blindfolded, was led into the temple of Demeter, located twenty furlongs outside the city. Afterward, two wolves guided him back to the starting point. During Rhampsinitos' reign, it was said that Egypt experienced orderly rule and prospered greatly. However, after his death, Cheops became king and brought about every kind of evil. He shut up all the temples, first depriving them of sacrifices, and then ordered all Egyptians to work for him.

Some were appointed to transport stones from the Arabian mountains to the Nile, while others were ordered to receive the stones after they had been carried over the river in boats and then transported to the Libyan mountains. A large workforce of 100,000 men worked continuously for three months at a time. After ten years of oppression, they built a causeway by drawing stones. The length of this causeway is five furlongs, its breadth ten fathoms, and its height eight fathoms at its highest point. It is made of stone, with figures carved upon it. For this, they said, he spent ten years. He caused a network of sepulchral chambers to be built on an island, which he reached by conducting a canal from the Nile.

The ancient Egyptian pharaohs built the pyramids in this order: first the highest parts, then the next section, and finally the lowest range near the ground. According to the Egyptians, Pharaoh Cheops ruled for 50 years before his death. His brother, Pharaoh Chephren, succeeded him and reigned for 56 years.

At Naucratis, it is said that courtesans often receive credit. The story I am referring to begins with a woman named Rhodopis, who became so famous that every Hellenic city came to know her name. After her, a woman named Archidice gained fame throughout Hellas, although she was less celebrated than the first woman. After redeeming Rhodopis, Charaxos returned to Mytilene. However, Sappho violently abused him in an ode. After Mykerinos, the priests said that Asychis became king of Egypt. He built a temple gateway for Hephaistos, which faced towards the rising sun. This was the most beautiful and largest of all the gateways. While the others had figures carved upon them and numerous ornaments, this one surpassed them all.

The cities were made even higher than before. They were first embanked by those who dug the channels during the reign of Sesostris, and then again during the reign of an Ethiopian ruler. As a result, they became very high. In contrast, other cities in Egypt also stood high, but particularly notable was the town at Bubastis, where the earth was piled up even higher. The final deliverance from the Ethiopian came about as follows: He fled away because he had seen in his sleep a vision, in which it seemed to him that a man appeared and counseled him to gather all the priests in Egypt and slay them. Having seen this dream, he said that it seemed to him that the gods were foretelling him this to furnish an occasion against him, so that he might commit an impious act with respect to religion and suffer some evil either from the gods or men. However, he would not do so; in truth, he said, the time had passed when it was prophesied to him that he would rule Egypt before leaving it.

When the Ethiopian had left Egypt, a blind man returned from the fen-country and resumed his rule. He had lived on an island created by piling up ashes and earth for fifty years. Whenever Egyptian visitors came to see him, bringing food as assigned to each one separately without the Ethiopian's knowledge, he would also ask them to bring some ashes as a gift. The priest of Hephaestus. This man was said to have neglected the warrior class of the Egyptians, considering they would have no need of them. He also put upon them other slights and took from them the yoke of corn-land that had been given as a special gift in the reigns of former kings.

The king of the Arabs and the Assyrians, Sanacharib, led a large army against Egypt. He trusted in these things seen in his sleep and took with him those Egyptians who were willing to follow him. They camped in Pelusion, which was the route by which the invasion came. Not a single warrior joined him, but rather shopkeepers, artisans, and merchants from the market. Then after they came, a swarm of field mice descended upon their enemies by night, devouring their arrows and bows, as well as the handles of their shields. The following day, they fled in disarray, with many falling due to their lack of defensive arms.

In the 11,300-year period, they claimed that no god appeared in human form. Neither before nor after this time did any of the subsequent Egyptian kings report such an event occurring. In this time they said that the sun had moved four times from its accustomed place of rising. Where he now sets, it had twice had its rising, and in the place from whence he now rises, it had twice had its setting. Meanwhile, nothing in Egypt had changed from its usual state: neither what comes from the earth nor what comes to them from the river, nor what concerns diseases or deaths. They led me into the temple's sanctuary, which is of great size. They counted the number, showing colossal wooden statues in the same quantity as they claimed. For each chief priest, an image of himself was set up during his lifetime. The priests then showed me these images and declared that each one was a son succeeding his father. They ascended through the series of images from the last deceased person's image until they revealed the total number.

Now πιρόμις (piromis) means in the tongue of Hellas "honourable and good man". From their declaration, it followed that those whose images were depicted had been of human form, far removed from being gods. However, in the time before these men, they claimed that gods ruled Egypt, separate from humans, and that one god held power at a time; the last of them to rule Egypt was Oros, son of Osiris, whom the Hellenes called Apollo. He was king over Egypt last, having deposed Typhon. Among the Hellenes, Heracles, Dionysos, and Pan are considered the youngest of the gods. However, with the Egyptians, Pan is an ancient god, one of the eight deities, whereas Heracles ranks second among the twelve gods, and Dionysos ranks third, being born from the offspring of the twelve gods. Now, Dionysus, son of Semele, daughter of Cadmus, was born about 1,600 years before my time. Heracles, son of Alcmene, was born about 900 years before my time. Pan, son of Hermes and Penelope, came into being later than the Trojan wars, which occurred around 800 years before my time.

I have already declared my opinion on this matter. After being freed from the priest of Hephaistos' reign, the Egyptians, who could not live without a king, established twelve kings over them, dividing Egypt into twelve parts. We speak of the chambers below based on what we've heard, while those above we saw ourselves and found to be works of extraordinary greatness.

The locals claimed that the lake had a hidden underground passage leading to the Syrtis region in Libya, situated on the western edge of the continent. The passage followed the slope of the mountain above Memphis. The digging of a lake in Egypt was attempted, but it was unsuccessful. The Egyptians had tried to carry the excavated earth to the Nile River during the day, but the river would naturally wash it away and scatter it. This lake was said to have been dug out.

Now that the twelve kings were ready to rule justly, but in the course of time it happened thus: After making a sacrifice in the temple of Hephaistos, they were about to make an offering on the last day of the feast. The chief-priest, bringing out the golden cups with which they had been accustomed to pour libations, mistakenly brought only eleven for the twelve kings. When he became king, it was his destiny to flee once more, this time due to the helmet, which led him and the eleven other kings into the marshy region. And he was not inclined to believe that bronze men would arrive to aid him; however, after a short time had elapsed, certain Ionians and Carians who had set sail in search of plunder were forced to land in Egypt. Once ashore, they donned their bronze armor and made their way to the fen-land, where they brought word to Psammetichos that bronze men had emerged from the sea and were pillaging the plain.

After King Amasis had them removed from that place, he established them at Memphis as a guard for himself against the Egyptians. Once settled in Egypt, we Hellenes learned about the events that took place there through our interactions with them. These men were the first foreigners to settle in Egypt, and their legacy continued until my time. Even after they left their homeland, remnants of their presence remained: the sheds where their ships were moored and the ruins of their houses still stood. Psammetichos obtained Egypt. I have previously mentioned the Oracle in Egypt, and now I shall recount its story, as it deserves to be told. This Oracle, sacred to Leto, is located in a great city near the mouth of the Nile River. The city, called Sebennytic, is situated as one sails up the river from the sea. The name of this city, where the Oracle is found, is Buto.

The island lies in a deep and broad lake beside the temple at Buto. According to Egyptian legend, the island is said to be a floating island. The Egyptians claimed that the island was floating. According to legend, Leto, one of the eight primordial gods, had received Apollo from Isis in the city of Buto, where she possessed an oracle. She then concealed Apollo within this island, which was said to be a floating island at the time. This occurred when Typhon, seeking the son of Osiris, arrived and searched everywhere for him. This man was the first to attempt the channel leading to the Erythraian Sea, which Darius the Persian later completed. The length of this passage is a voyage of four days, and its breadth was so dug that two triremes could sail side by side, driven by oars.

The channel runs above the city of Bubastis, near Patumos, an Arabian city. It enters the Erythraian Sea. The channel begins along the Egyptian plain's edge, toward Arabia, just north of the mountains that stretch opposite Memphis, where stone-quarries are located. Along the base of these mountains, the channel flows from west to east for a considerable distance. Afterward, it turns toward a hill break and moves toward the noon-day sun and the South Wind, ultimately reaching the Arabian gulf. In the place where the journey is least and shortest from the Northern Sea to the Southern Sea (also known as the Erythraian Sea), located between Mount Casius, which marks the boundary between Egypt and Syria, the distance to the Arabian Gulf is approximately a thousand furlongs. However, the channel itself is much longer due to its winding course. During the reign of Necos, while excavating it, twelve myriads of Egyptians lost their lives. Necos stopped digging in the middle of his work because the Oracle's words prevented him. The Oracle stated that Necos was working for a Barbarian, and the Egyptians consider anyone who does not speak their language to be a Barbarian. Necos, having ceased from his work on the channel, turned to waging war. He constructed triremes, some for use in the Northern Sea and others in the Arabian Gulf for the Erythraean Sea. The remains of these structures can still be seen today.

These ships he used when needed. He also engaged battle with the Syrians at Magdolos, where Necos defeated them. After this, he conquered Cadytis, a great city in Syria. The dress he wore during these conquests was dedicated to Apollo and sent to Branchidai of the Milesians. After this, he reigned for sixteen years and then brought his life to an end, handing over the kingdom to Psammis, his son. While Psammis was king of Egypt, men were sent by the Eleians who boasted that they had ordered the contest at Olympia in the most just and honourable manner possible. They thought that even the Egyptians, the wisest of men, could not find out anything beyond what was already added to their rules. When the Eleians arrived in Egypt, they stated their purpose. The king summoned the wisest Egyptians and shared with them what the Eleians intended to do regarding the contest. After hearing everything, the Egyptians asked if there was anything else they could learn from the Eleians that would be more just than what had already been said.

When Psammis became king of Egypt after just six years, he launched a campaign against Ethiopia. Shortly thereafter, he took his own life. His son, Apries, succeeded him as ruler of Egypt. This man became the most prosperous of all the kings up to that time, except for his forefather Psammetichos. He ruled for twenty-five years, leading an army against Sidon and engaging in a sea battle with the king of Tyre. It is fitting that evil should befall him. This occurred due to a particular issue that I will elaborate on further in the Libyan chronicles; for now, I will only touch upon it briefly.

Apries, having sent a great expedition against the Kyrenians, met with correspondingly great disaster. The Egyptians, considering him to blame for this revolt, turned against him, supposing that Apries had deliberately sent them out to certain calamity, in order that there might be a slaughter of them and he could rule more securely over the other Egyptians. Patarbemis both perceived his intention from what he said and also saw his preparations. He departed in haste, eager to inform the king as quickly as possible about the actions being taken: however, when he returned to Apries without Amasis, the king paid no heed to his words but was incensed by violent anger, ordering his ears and nose to be cut off. There are seven classes among the Egyptians. One class is that of the priests, another that of the warriors. The remaining classes include cowherds, swineherds, shopkeepers, interpreters, and boatmen.

This was a special privilege granted to all. Those who received it also had certain advantages. The men were not the same twice; that is to say, 1,000 Calasirians and 1,000 Hermotybians acted as bodyguards to the king each year. In addition to their land yokes, they received an allowance of five pounds of bread per man per day, two pounds of beef, and four half-pints of wine. When Apries led his foreign mercenaries and Amasis headed the entire Egyptian army as they approached each other in the city of Memphis, they clashed in battle. Although the foreign troops fought valiantly, they were ultimately defeated due to their numerical inferiority. Apries was said to believe that no god could make him stop ruling, as he thought the throne was firmly established.

There for some time he was kept in the palace. Amasis dealt well with him, but at last, since the Egyptians blamed him, saying that he had acted unjustly in keeping alive a foe who was both greatest enemy to themselves and to him. Therefore, he delivered Apries over to the Egyptians. They strangled him, and after that, they buried him in the burial-place of his fathers: this is in the temple of Athena, close to the sanctuary, on the left hand as you enter. Of the things I am more familiar with, I will not speak in detail; and while I know about the mystic rites of Demeter, which the Hellenes call _thesmophoria_, I will say nothing except what is fitting for a pious person to share. The daughters of Danaos brought this rite from Egypt and taught it to the women of the Pelasgians. Later, when all the inhabitants of Peloponnese were displaced by the Dorians, the rite was lost. Only those who remained behind among the Peloponnesians – specifically the Arcadians – preserved it.

The Egyptians initially viewed Amasis with disdain, as he was a commoner without a noble lineage. However, Amasis eventually gained their respect through his intelligence rather than arrogance. He won the Egyptians over to himself, earning their consent as subjects. His approach to governance was as follows: In the morning hours, until market opening, he handled cases with goodwill; afterwards, he devoted his time to drinking and jesting with his companions, displaying a carefree and lighthearted nature. This artifact lies beside the temple entrance. The temple's architects did not include it in their design because, according to legend, while they were constructing the building, the chief craftsman expressed frustration at the prolonged work process. Amasis II took this as a warning and forbade them from proceeding further with its inclusion.

During the reign of Amasis, Egypt experienced unprecedented prosperity. The country benefited from an abundance of goods coming from the Nile River as well as trade with neighboring lands. This period saw a significant increase in the number of thriving towns throughout the land, with estimates suggesting around 20,000 inhabited settlements. Amasis provided the Greeks with a substantial amount of materials, including alum, which was obtained from both Egypt and other locations. He gifted them one thousand talents' worth of alum, while the Egyptians, who were also known as the Hellenes, contributed twenty pounds of silver. Also with the people of Kyrene, Amasis made an agreement for friendship and alliance. He resolved to marry a wife from there, either because he desired a wife of Hellenic race or, apart from that, due to his friendship for the people of Kyrene. Whatever the reason, he married, some say the daughter of Battos, others of Arkesilaos, and others of Critobulos, a man of reputation among the citizens; her name was Ladike.

At Samos, he dedicated offerings due to his friendship with Polycrates, son of Aiakes. In Lindos, he made a similar gesture without any guest-friendship ties, as the temple of Athena was said to have been founded by the daughters of Danaus, who had arrived on land during their flight from the sons of Egypt.
